text:
The Great Balling Formula Part 2 This article continues the story of evolution of the Great Balling Formula, looking at the development of the factors for alcohol and those attenuation quotients that had changed in Balling’s revised edition of 1854 from the original version of 1846, using them to introduce, from a contemporary brewing perspective, a modified version of the formula, which no longer makes use of Tabarie’s formula.

Apart from reviewing the underlying physical principle of the attenuation quotient q recently under discussion in more detail and commenting on the application potential for the booming craft brewing sector, where prompt and easy-to-calculate assessment of important beer properties is demanded, a physical quantity that has reliably performed in the brewing industry for more than 150 years is discussed. Given its long-standing stability in commercial application, it is highly unlikely that this decades long established empirical formula of brewing science will be modified by introducing the more physically consistent versions for use in brewery practice in the immediate future. In lieu of costly, time-consuming analytical measurements to determine the content of various nutri-tions in beer that varies largely between different beer styles, communication of real extract and real alcohol content, both easily accessible values of particular relevance to the end consumer of a beer, in a well understandable and therefore also effective manner inform regarding the approximate content of nutritional constituents and calories in beer.
